question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
we performed
a meta-analysis to evaluate the correlation of the
clinical manifestations and laboratory disorders in
patients with familial and sporadic-onset SLE.

Studies meeting the following criteria were
included in this review: (a) studies evaluating the
differences between familial lupus and sporadic
lupus with clinical and serological manifestations;
(b) cohort studies designed with familial lupus; and
(c) studies with genetic epidemiology sufficient data
available to calculate the odds ratio (OR) with 95%
confidence interval (95% CI). Studies were
excluded in any of the following cases: (a) not a
human study; (b) not an original study; (c) cohort
designs leading to outcome of interest and (d) study
not pertaining to the clinical or immunologic features
of familial SLE.

question: Were eligibility criteria unambiguous?
answers:
Studies meeting the following criteria were
included in this review: (a) studies evaluating the
differences between familial lupus and sporadic
lupus with clinical and serological manifestations;
(b) cohort studies designed with familial lupus; and
(c) studies with genetic epidemiology sufficient data
available to calculate the odds ratio (OR) with 95%
confidence interval (95% CI). Studies were
excluded in any of the following cases: (a) not a
human study; (b) not an original study; (c) cohort
designs leading to outcome of interest and (d) study
not pertaining to the clinical or immunologic features
of familial SLE.

question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria
answers:
Studies were excluded in any of the following cases: (a) not a
human study; (b) not an original study; (c) cohort
designs leading to outcome of interest and (d) study
not pertaining to the clinical or immunologic features
of familial SLE

If several articles
were published by the same authors using the
same patients' data, only studies with the largest
sample size or the most recent study were included.

The remaining 40 articles underwent a full
text review, and an additional 34 articles were
excluded: Twenty-five had no comparative data on
familial and sporadic SLE, eight focused on a specialized
subset of patients (only female patients or
patients with kidney diseases), and one utilized the
same study population.

question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers: were searched without language restrictions

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers:
The literature databases
of PubMed, EMBASE, Web of Science and China
National Knowledge Infrastructure (CNKI)

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers:
The references in the identified or related articles
were then manually reviewed to search other relevant
citations

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers:
The search strategies were based on the following
terms: ''lupus,'' ''SLE,'' or ''systemic lupus erythematosus,''
combined with ''familial,'' ''sporadic,''
''familial history,'' ''families,'' or ''genetic epidemiology.''
Thesaurus terms and free texts were used

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers: were searched without language restrictions

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers:
Two reviewers separately included papers using
the inclusion and exclusion criteria

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers:
Relevant data
from each eligible article was extracted independently
by two authors using a standardized data
extraction form: the first author's name, publication
date, location, total number of SLE patients,
sex ratio, the mean age at inclusion, the mean age
onset, clinical manifestations (expressed as either
the number or percentage of familial and sporadic
patients). Discrepancies were resolved by group
discussion.

question: Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
answers:
Table 1 lists the demographics of patients included
in the retrieved studies. The gender ratio
(female:male) varied from 7:1 to 15:1 among studies
with no difference between familial and sporadic
SLE as reported by four of six studies.15,16,18,19
The mean ages at inclusion and the onset ages listed
in three studies were comparable between patients
with and without family history of SLE. In addition,
Abutiban et al. analyzed disease duration
and found no difference between the two groups.1

question: Were all relevant study results collected for use in the synthesis?
answers: Figure 2 Forest plot of the association of familial SLE history with the clinical and laboratory parameters.

question: Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
answers:
The quality assessment of included studies was
based on the Newcastle-Ottawa assessment (NOS)
scale.7 The criteria for selection assessment were
modified according to the Critical Appraisal Skills
Program (CASP)8 and Quality Assessment Tool for
Systematic Reviews of Observational Studies
(QATSO)9 to make the subcategories more applicable
to grouped cohort studies. Total score varies
from 0 to nine points, with nine representing the
highest quality.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
answers: nan

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers:

SLE cases and 1405 sporadic cases investigated
the association of familial history with mucocutaneous

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers: nan

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers:
Both the chi-square-based Q-statistic test and I2
were used to assess the heterogeneity among studies.
The I2 value represented the proportion of
variation due to heterogeneities in studies. The heterogeneity
level was classified into four categories:
unimportant (0-40%), moderate (30-60%),
substantial (50-90%) and considerable (75-100%)
heterogeneity.11 If the p value of the heterogeneity
Q-statistic0.10 or I250%, the random-effects
model was selected.12 Otherwise, a fixed-effects
model was adopted.

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
The overall meta-analyses showed
that familial SLE was negatively associated with
photosensitivity (OR 0.73, 95% CI 0.60-0.89,
p 1/40.002) but with a moderate heterogeneity
(I2 1/435%, pHeterogeneity 1/40.17) (Figure 2(a)).

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
answers:
Publication bias was investigated
by funnel plot if there were more than
10 studies involved

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
Of the six
studies, one scored six points, and two scored seven
points, and the other scored nine points. All studies
defined SLE cases with the American College of
Rheumatology (ACR) criteria (1982 or 1997 revision)
and described the characteristics of patients in
that context. Data from all of SLE patients and their
affected relatives were collected by reviewing their
medical records if available or by personal interview
and blood collection if the data were missing.

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
First, with respect to the genetic component, the
concordance rate was higher in monozygotic
twins (>20%) than in dizygotic twins and other
full siblings (2-5%), indicating a linkage between
the degree of relatives and genetic factors.25-27 In
this meta-analysis, there were not enough data to
analysis whether the lupus-related parameters
varied with the degree of relatives. Second, the
sex ratios were different in the included articles.
Sexual hormones are critical for SLE development
and associated with SLE manifestations. It was
reported that female patients were highly associated
with alopecia, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, arthritis,
malar rash, lupus anticoagulant level, and
low level of C3, while male patients were more
likely to have renal involvement, serositis and
pleurisies, thrombocytopenia, and serum level of
anti-dsDNA antibody.28 Thus, the varied sexual
ratios in each study could cause some bias. Third,
the mean onset ages were not specified in three articles.
The age of onset has been reported to be
related to clinical manifestations. Patients with
childhood-onset lupus were commonly found to
have fever, malar rash, hematologic disorders
(hemolytic anemia and thrombocytopenia), renal
involvement and lymphadenopathy.29 Last, it is
important to note that a couple of the largest studies
had lower quality but they would count as
higher weight because of the large sizes.

question: Was the relevance of identified studies to the review's research question appropriately considered?
answers:
Thus, to better understand the
association of genetic factors with the manifestations
and the pathogenesis of SLE, we performed
a meta-analysis to evaluate the correlation of the
clinical manifestations and laboratory disorders in
patients with familial and sporadic-onset SLE

Table 1 Characteristics of included studies

All studies
defined SLE cases with the American College of
Rheumatology (ACR) criteria (1982 or 1997 revision)
and described the characteristics of patients in
that context. Data from all of SLE patients and their
affected relatives were collected by reviewing their
medical records if available or by personal interview
and blood collection if the data were missing.
